responses.text: The target is a first-person WIRED feature by science journalist Sarah Scoles, published September 20, 2019, recounting the on-the-ground scene at the "Storm Area 51" gatherings in Rachel and Hiko, Nevada.
Its central framing is that the gates of Area 51 were not actually "stormed" and that the event functioned mainly as a meme-driven photo op rather than a sincere attempt to find aliens or UFOs.

Scoles is an established science writer who covers space and is the author of UFO-culture book "They Are Already Here: UFO Culture and Why We See Saucers," which the article explicitly references and which other outlets corroborate as her work.

The article's claim that the joke Facebook event seeded the entire phenomenon is confirmed by multiple outlets that name Matty Roberts, a college student from Bakersfield, California, as the creator of "Storm Area 51, They Can't Stop All of Us" on June 27, 2019.

The article's depiction of low turnout relative to online interest matches independent reporting: more than 2 million people RSVP'd on Facebook, but only about 75 people were at the Rachel back gate at the 3 a.m. "raid" hour, and festival crowds peaked at roughly 3,000 in Rachel rather than the feared 30,000.

The article's account of a chaotic, scaled-back "A'le'Inn Stock" is consistent with reporting that Roberts split from Little A'Le'Inn owner Connie West, sent a cease-and-desist, and moved his own "Alienstock" event to Las Vegas, while a competing "Area 51 Basecamp" in Hiko also drew far fewer attendees than expected.

Scoles's portrayal of law enforcement as friendly and largely permissive, with deputies even letting visitors walk up to the gate, lines up with other on-scene reporting that described a "mostly peaceful" atmosphere, few arrests, and officers joking with attendees.

The article's framing of Bob Lazar as the basis for Area 51 alien-conspiracy theories—calling them "based on not much but strange aerodynamic sightings and the word of a man named Bob Lazar"—is well supported: Lazar's S-4 and reverse-engineering claims have no public documentary support, his educational records cannot be confirmed at MIT or Caltech, and he has criminal convictions for prostitution-related and illegal-chemical offenses, with Wikipedia summarizing that he "has no evidence of alien life or technology."

The article's offhand timeline note that Highway 375 was renamed the Extraterrestrial Highway around the time "Independence Day" came out checks out: Nevada formally designated State Route 375 the Extraterrestrial Highway in early 1996, with an April 1996 unveiling featuring Gov. Bob Miller and "Independence Day" cast members, the same year the film was released.

Missing context: the piece is a personal, scene-driven feature rather than a comprehensive incident report, so it does not mention the small number of arrests and detentions documented elsewhere, including roughly five arrests over the weekend mostly for trespassing.

Additional context the article does not emphasize: Lincoln County officials estimated the event could cost taxpayers as much as $250,000, mostly for added law enforcement, and at least two music/festival components ended early due to low attendance.

Source quality is high: WIRED is an established Condé Nast technology and science magazine with editorial standards, and the byline is a credentialed space and UFO-culture journalist whose reporting on this exact subject area is corroborated by her book and other reputable outlets.

Bottom line: the article is best read as a credible, lightly opinionated eyewitness essay whose factual scaffolding—creator, dates, locations, turnout, festival collapse, law-enforcement posture, and skepticism toward Bob Lazar's alien claims—is consistent with independent reporting available at the time and since.
